agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The courier assignment system faces a fundamental CAP theorem tension: the product team demands strong consistency (no double-assignment) AND high availability (survive node failures) simultaneously. These are partially in conflict. The architecture resolves this by treating consistency as the non-negotiable constraint (double-assigning a courier is a business-critical invariant), accepting brief unavailability during failover (seconds, not minutes), and achieving 200ms latency through in-memory state with persistent backing. Given a 2-engineer team, the design favors a single-writer architecture with a hot standby over distributed consensus, keeping operational complexity manageable.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Assignment Service","responsibility":"Receives order-placed events, selects the optimal available courier, and atomically marks them as assigned. Owns the in-memory availability index for sub-200ms decisions.","boundaries":"Owns courier availability state. Does NOT own courier profiles, order lifecycle, or routing/ETA. Single-writer: only one active instance holds the lock at any time."},{"name":"Courier Availability Store","responsibility":"Persistent source of truth for courier availability status. Backed by PostgreSQL with row-level locking. Feeds the in-memory index on startup and failover.","boundaries":"Stores availability state only (courier_id, status, current_order_id, last_heartbeat, zone). Not a general courier profile store."},{"name":"API Gateway / Order Ingress","responsibility":"Receives order placement requests, validates payload, and publishes OrderPlaced events. Returns assignment result synchronously within the 200ms budget.","boundaries":"Thin layer. No business logic. Routes to Assignment Service via synchronous call for latency budget."},{"name":"Courier Heartbeat Receiver","responsibility":"Ingests periodic location and availability updates from courier mobile apps. Updates both the persistent store and pushes changes to the Assignment Service's in-memory index.","boundaries":"Write-path only. Does not make assignment decisions."},{"name":"Failover Controller","responsibility":"Monitors the active Assignment Service instance. Promotes the hot standby if the primary becomes unresponsive. Uses PostgreSQL advisory locks for leader election.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern only. Does not participate in business logic."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Assignment Service","contract":"POST /assign { order_id, pickup_location, delivery_location } → { courier_id, estimated_pickup } | 503 if no leader","coupling_type":"synchronous RPC (latency-critical path)"},{"from":"Assignment Service","to":"Courier Availability Store","contract":"SELECT FOR UPDATE / UPDATE on courier_availability table. Atomic read-modify-write per assignment.","coupling_type":"synchronous DB call (write-through)"},{"from":"Courier Heartbeat Receiver","to":"Courier Availability Store","contract":"UPSERT courier_availability SET location, last_heartbeat WHERE courier_id = ?","coupling_type":"asynchronous (best-effort, high frequency)"},{"from":"Courier Heartbeat Receiver","to":"Assignment Service","contract":"Push via internal event/channel to update in-memory index. If Assignment Service is unreachable, heartbeats still persist to DB.","coupling_type":"async push (protocol: gRPC stream or Redis pub/sub)"},{"from":"Failover Controller","to":"Assignment Service","contract":"Health check endpoint GET /health every 1s. On 3 consecutive failures, trigger standby promotion via pg_advisory_lock.","coupling_type":"infrastructure coupling (health monitoring)"}],"data_flow":"1. Order placed → API Gateway → synchronous call to Assignment Service. 2. Assignment Service checks in-memory availability index (sub-ms), selects best courier by proximity/load. 3. Assignment Service executes SELECT FOR UPDATE on courier row in PostgreSQL → verifies still available → UPDATE status='assigned', current_order_id=X → commits. 4. If DB confirms, respond with courier_id (total <200ms). If DB rejects (row already locked/changed), retry with next-best courier. 5. Courier heartbeats flow continuously into the Availability Store and are pushed to the in-memory index. 6. On delivery completion, courier app sends status update → Availability Store marks courier available → pushes to in-memory index.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Single-Writer with Hot Standby","rationale":"Eliminates distributed consensus complexity while providing strong consistency. Only one instance can assign couriers at a time, enforced by PostgreSQL advisory locks. A 2-engineer team can operate this confidently."},{"pattern":"Write-Through Cache","rationale":"In-memory index provides sub-ms reads for courier selection. Every assignment writes through to PostgreSQL before confirming. Cache is rebuilt from DB on failover—no stale state risk."},{"pattern":"Optimistic Selection with Pessimistic Commit","rationale":"Courier selection uses the fast in-memory index (optimistic), but the actual assignment uses SELECT FOR UPDATE (pessimistic) to guarantee no double-assignment. Best of both worlds for latency + consistency."},{"pattern":"Leader Election via Database Advisory Locks","rationale":"PostgreSQL pg_advisory_lock provides leader election without introducing ZooKeeper/etcd. Keeps the infrastructure footprint minimal for a small team."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Courier Assignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["CourierAvailability (aggregate root: courier_id, status enum [available|assigned|offline], current_order_id, location, zone, last_heartbeat). Invariant: a courier with status=assigned cannot be assigned to another order.)","AssignmentRecord (entity: assignment_id, order_id, courier_id, assigned_at, status. Immutable audit trail.)"]},{"name":"Order Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Order (aggregate root: order_id, status, pickup, delivery). Upstream context—publishes OrderPlaced events that trigger assignment.)"]},{"name":"Courier Profile","classification":"generic","aggregates":["CourierProfile (aggregate root: courier_id, name, vehicle_type, rating). Rarely changes. Read-only from Assignment context.)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderPlaced; Assignment consumes it. Assignment does not dictate Order schema."},{"upstream":"Courier Profile","downstream":"Courier Assignment","relationship":"Conformist. Assignment reads courier metadata (vehicle type, rating) but does not modify it. Conforms to Profile's schema."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single-writer architecture over distributed consensus (e.g., Raft-based service)","gains":"Dramatically simpler operations, debugging, and mental model. 2 engineers can fully own it. No split-brain risk. Strong consistency is trivially guaranteed.","sacrifices":"During leader failover (typically 1-5 seconds), assignments are unavailable. Throughput is bounded by single-node capacity (sufficient for most logistics platforms—thousands of assignments/sec).","alternatives_considered":["Raft-based distributed assignment service: higher availability during failover but dramatically more complex to operate and debug. Overkill for team size.","Distributed locking with Redis Redlock: tempting but Redlock has known safety issues under clock skew and network partitions. Not suitable for a strong consistency requirement."]},{"decision":"PostgreSQL as both persistence and coordination layer","gains":"Single infrastructure dependency. Advisory locks, row-level locking, ACID transactions all come free. Team already likely knows Postgres. Streaming replication provides the hot standby.","sacrifices":"Postgres becomes a single point of failure (mitigated by synchronous replication to standby). Not horizontally scalable for writes (acceptable at logistics scale).","alternatives_considered":["Separate coordination service (etcd/ZooKeeper) + DB: adds operational burden for marginal benefit at this scale.","DynamoDB with conditional writes: strong consistency possible but vendor lock-in and less flexible querying."]},{"decision":"Synchronous assignment (request-response) over async event-driven","gains":"Meets 200ms latency requirement with clear timeout semantics. Caller knows immediately if assignment succeeded. Simpler error handling.","sacrifices":"Tighter coupling between API Gateway and Assignment Service. Assignment Service must be available for orders to complete.","alternatives_considered":["Async with callback: lower coupling but adds latency, complexity, and makes the 200ms SLA harder to guarantee."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Courier selection algorithm","evolution_path":"Currently simple proximity-based selection from in-memory index. Can evolve to ML-based scoring, multi-factor optimization (rating, capacity, historical performance) without changing the assignment flow or consistency model."},{"area":"Geographic sharding","evolution_path":"If throughput exceeds single-writer capacity, partition by geographic zone. Each zone gets its own single-writer Assignment Service. Couriers belong to exactly one zone at a time. No cross-zone coordination needed."},{"area":"Assignment strategy","evolution_path":"Can add batch assignment (assign N orders simultaneously for optimal global allocation) as an enhancement. The write-through + pessimistic commit pattern supports this."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Single-writer constraint","reason":"The entire consistency guarantee depends on exactly one Assignment Service instance holding the advisory lock. This is intentionally rigid—relaxing it requires a fundamentally different consistency model."},{"area":"PostgreSQL as coordination backbone","reason":"Leader election, persistence, and consistency all flow through Postgres. Migrating away would require replacing all three concerns simultaneously. This is acceptable because Postgres is battle-tested and the team is small."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Failover window causes order assignment failures for 1-5 seconds","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Client-side retry with exponential backoff (3 retries over 2 seconds). API Gateway returns 503 during failover so callers know to retry. Most logistics workflows tolerate a few seconds of delay. Monitor failover frequency—if it happens often, investigate root cause rather than shortening failover time."},{"risk":"In-memory index diverges from PostgreSQL during network partition between Heartbeat Receiver and Assignment Service","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Assignment Service always validates against PostgreSQL before committing. The in-memory index is an optimization for selection, not the source of truth. Stale index means suboptimal courier choice, not incorrect assignment."},{"risk":"PostgreSQL synchronous replication latency eats into the 200ms budget","severity":"high","mitigation":"Measure replication latency. If it exceeds 20ms consistently, switch to asynchronous replication and accept the (very small) risk of losing the most recent assignment on primary failure. In practice, same-datacenter synchronous replication adds 1-5ms."},{"risk":"Advisory lock not released on process crash, blocking standby promotion","severity":"medium","mitigation":"PostgreSQL automatically releases advisory locks when the session disconnects. Ensure the Assignment Service uses a dedicated connection for the advisory lock, and the Failover Controller monitors both application health AND the Postgres session."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Build the Assignment Service as a single Go or Java service with an in-memory concurrent map for courier availability. Use PostgreSQL with SELECT FOR UPDATE for the assignment transaction. Deploy with a hot standby using pg_advisory_lock for leader election. Phase 2: Add the Heartbeat Receiver as a separate lightweight service. Phase 3: Add geographic zone support when throughput requires it.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/assignment-service/main.go — entry point, leader election loop, HTTP server","src/assignment-service/assigner/assigner.go — core assignment logic: select from index, pessimistic commit to DB","src/assignment-service/index/availability_index.go — in-memory spatial index of available couriers (R-tree or simple zone-based map)","src/assignment-service/leader/election.go — pg_advisory_lock based leader election with health endpoint","src/assignment-service/db/migrations/001_courier_availability.sql — CREATE TABLE courier_availability (courier_id UUID PK, status TEXT, current_order_id UUID, location POINT, zone TEXT, last_heartbeat TIMESTAMPTZ, version INT)","src/assignment-service/db/migrations/002_assignment_log.sql — CREATE TABLE assignment_log (id UUID PK, order_id UUID, courier_id UUID, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT)","src/heartbeat-receiver/main.go — lightweight service accepting courier location updates","docker-compose.yml — Postgres primary + standby, Assignment Service primary + standby, Heartbeat Receiver"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Repository pattern for DB access. Keep the assignment algorithm behind an interface (AssignmentStrategy) so it can be swapped. Use structured logging with correlation IDs (order_id) for every assignment attempt. Add Prometheus metrics: assignment_latency_ms, assignment_success_total, assignment_retry_total, leader_failover_total.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use Redis Redlock for distributed locking—it does not provide the safety guarantees needed for strong consistency under network partitions.","Do NOT make the in-memory index the source of truth—always validate against PostgreSQL before confirming an assignment.","Do NOT use async messaging on the critical assignment path—it makes the 200ms SLA unpredictable.","Do NOT over-engineer for horizontal scale on day one—a single PostgreSQL instance handles thousands of writes per second, which covers most logistics platforms.","Do NOT forget to set statement_timeout on the assignment query—a slow query will blow the 200ms budget and cascade."]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_constraints","description":"The 200ms latency target, strong consistency, and high availability form a CAP-adjacent tension. The design resolves this by accepting brief unavailability during failover (1-5 seconds). The product team should explicitly confirm this trade-off is acceptable. Additionally, throughput requirements (orders per second) are unspecified—the single-writer design handles thousands/sec but has a ceiling. If the platform operates at tens of thousands of orders/sec globally, geographic sharding becomes mandatory from day one rather than a future evolution.","options":["Accept 1-5 second unavailability during failover (recommended)","Require zero-downtime failover, which necessitates distributed consensus and significantly more operational complexity","Accept eventual consistency (allow rare double-assignments, reconcile after), which simplifies everything but violates requirement #2"],"recommendation":"Accept brief failover unavailability. It aligns with the 2-engineer team constraint and the nature of logistics (a few seconds delay on a rare failover is operationally insignificant compared to physical delivery times)."}],"confidence":{"level":60,"high_confidence_areas":["Single-writer with pessimistic DB locking is the correct consistency model for this problem","PostgreSQL advisory locks for leader election is well-proven and appropriate for team size","In-memory index with write-through is the right latency optimization pattern"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 1-5 second failover window is acceptable to the product team","Throughput requirements—design assumes sub-10K assignments/sec","Whether the team has PostgreSQL operational expertise for synchronous replication setup"],"assumptions":["The platform operates within a single geographic region or can be partitioned by region","Courier count is in the thousands to tens of thousands, not millions","The 200ms budget is measured at the API Gateway, including network hops to/from the Assignment Service","PostgreSQL synchronous replication latency is under 5ms (same-datacenter deployment)"]}}
